title: IF-RewardBench: Benchmarking Judge Models for Instruction-Following Evaluation
authors: Bosi Wen, Yilin Niu, Cunxiang Wang, Xiaoying Ling, Ying Zhang, Pei Ke, Hongning Wang, Minlie Huang (CoAI Group, Tsinghua University; Zhipu AI; University of Electronic Science and Technology of China)
venue: arXiv 2603.04738, v2 (April 16, 2026); v1 submitted March 5, 2026; arXiv Comments field lists ACL 2026
claim: Current judge models (LLM-as-judge and dedicated reward models) are unreliable evaluators of instruction-following: on IF-RewardBench, even the best proprietary model (Gemini-3-Pro) reaches only Kendall tau_b = 0.609 in ranking responses by instruction-following quality, well below the human baseline of 0.755, and all dedicated reward models score below 0.2. The paper argues this gap stems from prior benchmarks' narrow single-turn/code-verifiable-constraint coverage and oversimplified pairwise/BoN evaluation paradigms, which IF-RewardBench addresses via a 'preference graph' of Pareto-dominance-derived pairwise preferences over multiple (m=8) responses per instruction, evaluated with both a pointwise constraint-checklist paradigm and a listwise ranking paradigm.
novelty: Authors claim three advantages over prior judge-model benchmarks (LLMBar, InfoBench, IFBench, PPE-IF, RewardBench-2-IF; Table 1, Sec 1): (1) Comprehensive Coverage -- 842 instructions spanning single-turn interaction, multi-turn interaction, and system-prompt steerability, with a taxonomy of 7 primary/54 secondary constraint categories and 4 constraint composition types (Single, And, Chain, Selection, plus Nested combinations), versus prior benchmarks' narrow code-verifiable/And-only constraints (Sec 1, Sec 3.2.1, Appendix A); (2) Realistic (listwise) Evaluation Paradigm -- a preference graph over all pairwise Pareto-dominance relations among m=8 responses per instruction, replacing winner-take-all pairwise/BoN selection (Sec 1, Sec 3.1.1-3.2.2); (3) Reliability -- multi-annotator human verification with Cohen's Kappa 0.67 (initial) / 0.87 (cross-validation), retaining only unanimous-agreement preference relations (71.2% of constructed pairs) (Sec 3.2.2, Sec 3.3, Appendix E.3). The paper also newly demonstrates IF-RewardBench correlates significantly more strongly with downstream Best-of-N sampling performance than existing benchmarks (Sec 4.2, Fig. 7).

protocol.description: IF-RewardBench evaluates JUDGE models, not generation models under an interactive loop with the task-solver. Two single-inference-pass tasks are defined per instruction (Sec 3.1.1): (1) Constraint Assessment -- given instruction I (possibly with system prompt + conversation history), a constraint checklist {c_k}, and one response y, the judge outputs a binary judgment j_k in {0,1} per constraint in ONE inference pass over the whole checklist (Appendix G.2: 'requires LLMs to evaluate the following of all constraints in the given checklist within a single inference pass'); quality score r_y = (1/n) * sum(j_k) (Eq. 1). (2) Overall Assessment -- given instruction I and a set of responses {y_i}, the judge produces quality scores or pairwise comparison verdicts (Sec 3.1.1); implemented via all-pairs comparison + ELO scoring per RRM (Sec 4.1, Appendix G.2). Judge models see the full system prompt, full conversation history, the final-round user instruction, the (candidate) response(s), and (for constraint assessment) the full constraint checklist all at once -- there is no multi-turn feedback loop with the judge; each judgment is a single forward pass over a single fully-specified prompt (Appendix C prompt templates, Tables 19-20). For overall/pairwise assessment the exact prompt (Table 21, MT-Bench-style) is quoted: 'Please act as an impartial judge and evaluate the quality of the responses provided by two AI assistants... Begin your evaluation by comparing the two responses and provide a short explanation... output your final verdict... [[A]] ... [[B]]'. For constraint assessment the exact prompt (Table 20; Table 19 is instead the instruction-decomposition prompt) instructs: 'Your task is to think carefully and provide a detailed analysis of whether the final round assistant response follows each constraint on the checklist... You must strictly output the analysis and judgment for each constraint in the following format: [The Start of Constraint N] ... Judgment: [[The AI assistant's response follows this constraint]] or [[The AI assistant's response does not follow this constraint]]'. No demos/few-shot exemplars are shown in the quoted core prompts for constraint assessment or pairwise comparison; in-context examples ARE used in the checklist-revision prompt (Table 15, '{examples}') and the quality-scoring prompt (Table 17, '{in_context_examples}') used during DATASET CONSTRUCTION, and in the human instruction-following annotation guideline (Table 16, '{in-context examples}'); the difficulty-scoring prompt (Table 18) and instruction-decomposition prompt (Table 19) contain NO in-context-examples placeholder, not necessarily in the main judge-model evaluation prompts.
protocol.note: This is a benchmark for judge/reward models scoring already-generated responses, not a benchmark of a generator model solving stacked constraints itself. There is no interactive multi-turn protocol between the paper's judge models and a task; 'multi-turn' in this paper refers to the INSTRUCTION TYPE (conversation history as input context), not an interactive evaluation loop.

representations: unverified — missing from extraction (the local extraction contains no ablation comparing alternative input/representation formats for the instruction or checklist itself, e.g., JSON vs. natural-language checklist, beyond the fixed prompt templates in Appendix C). The paper does report one relevant format-adjacent finding: constraint-level (checklist-guided) scoring outperforms overall/pairwise comparison without a checklist for general LLM judges (Sec 4.2 'Main Results', third bullet: 'general LLMs perform worse on overall assessment than constraint assessment, likely due to the difficulty of identifying constraints within complex instructions'), i.e., providing the decomposed constraint checklist as part of the observed input measurably improves judge accuracy versus giving only the raw instruction.

models.name: Gemini-3-Pro
strategies: prompted-as-judge (single-pass constraint checklist evaluation / pairwise comparison)
models.key_results: Constraint assessment (Table 3): Avg P-F1 0.909, N-F1 0.681, tau_b 0.609 (best of all judges; human = 0.755 tau_b).

models.name: Gemini-3-Flash
models.key_results: Constraint assessment (Table 3): Avg tau_b 0.572. Overall assessment/pairwise+ELO (Table 4): Avg tau_b 0.513 (best among general LLMs on overall assessment). On the Appendix G.3 cross-benchmark comparison (Table 6, IHEval-sourced subset, overall assessment): tau_b 0.161 -- this is the SECOND-highest score in that subset, behind GPT-5-mini's 0.211 (most other judges score negative, i.e. worse than random, on this subset). Inference-time-scaling not tested on this model (only Qwen-3-32B and GLM-4.6 tested, Table 5).

models.name: GPT-5-mini
models.key_results: Constraint assessment (Table 3): Avg tau_b 0.519. Overall assessment (Table 4): Avg tau_b 0.456. On the Appendix G.3 cross-benchmark comparison (Table 6, IHEval-sourced subset, overall assessment): tau_b 0.211, the TOP score in that subset (ahead of Gemini-3-Flash's 0.161; most other judges score negative there).

models.name: DeepSeek-V3.2
models.key_results: Constraint assessment (Table 3): Avg tau_b 0.395 (top open-source alongside GLM-4.6). Overall assessment (Table 4): Avg tau_b 0.288.

models.name: GLM-4.6
strategies: prompted-as-judge; also tested with/without long-chain (thinking) reasoning and self-consistency (K=1..9 majority voting)
models.key_results: Constraint assessment (Table 3): Avg tau_b 0.422. Overall assessment (Table 4): Avg tau_b 0.270. Inference-time scaling (Table 5): baseline CA 0.422/OA 0.270; w/o Thinking CA 0.284 (-32.7%) OA 0.253 (-6.3%); w/ Maj@5 CA 0.484 (+14.7%) OA 0.302 (+11.9%); performance saturates beyond K=7 (Sec 4.3).

models.name: GLM-4.5-Air
models.key_results: Constraint assessment (Table 3): Avg tau_b 0.308. Overall assessment (Table 4): Avg tau_b 0.148.

models.name: QwQ-32B
models.key_results: Constraint assessment (Table 3): Avg tau_b 0.356. Overall assessment (Table 4): Avg tau_b 0.178.

models.name: Qwen-3-32B
strategies: prompted-as-judge; tested with/without thinking mode and self-consistency
models.key_results: Constraint assessment (Table 3): Avg tau_b 0.285. Overall assessment (Table 4): Avg tau_b 0.129. Inference-time scaling (Table 5): baseline CA 0.285/OA 0.129; w/o Thinking CA 0.253 (-11.2%) OA 0.106 (-17.8%); w/ Maj@7 CA 0.298 (+4.6%) OA 0.163 (+26.4%).

models.name: Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct
models.key_results: Constraint assessment (Table 3): Avg tau_b 0.238. Overall assessment (Table 4): Avg tau_b 0.054.

models.name: Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct
models.key_results: Constraint assessment (Table 3): Avg tau_b 0.089 (weakest general LLM tested; Single-Turn tau_b only 0.021).

models.name: Qwen-2.5-72B-Instruct
models.key_results: Constraint assessment (Table 3): Avg tau_b 0.181. Overall assessment (Table 4): Avg tau_b 0.048.

models.name: Human annotators (baseline)
strategies: manual constraint checking per checklist (task definition, not a model)
models.key_results: Constraint assessment (Table 3): Avg P-F1 0.923, N-F1 0.744, tau_b 0.755 (upper reference point for judge models).

models.name: Dedicated reward models (Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B, Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct-RM-RB2, LMUnit-Qwen-2.5-72B, Qwen2.5-Math-RM-72B, InternLM2-20B-Reward, RRM-32B, RM-R1-DeepSeek-Distilled-Qwen-32B, M-Prometheus-14B)
strategies: discriminative or generative reward-model scoring (overall assessment only; not applicable to constraint assessment)
models.key_results: Overall assessment (Table 4): all fail to exceed tau_b 0.2; best is Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B at Avg 0.133; weakest M-Prometheus-14B at 0.020.

headline_numbers: 842 instructions, 6,011 responses (from 16 response-generation LLMs), 9,145 preference relations total; avg 7.14 responses and 10.86 preference relations per preference graph (Table 2, Sec 3.3).
Best judge (Gemini-3-Pro) achieves only Kendall tau_b = 0.609 on constraint-assessment ranking, vs. human baseline tau_b = 0.755 (Table 3, Abstract, Sec 4.2).

Top open-source LLM judges (GLM-4.6, DeepSeek-V3.2) remain at or below tau_b ~ 0.4 (0.422 and 0.395 resp., Table 3); all dedicated reward models fail to exceed tau_b = 0.2 on overall assessment (Table 4, best 0.133).

General LLMs perform worse on Overall Assessment (no checklist given) than Constraint Assessment (checklist given): e.g. Gemini-3-Flash 0.513 (OA) vs 0.572 (CA average); GLM-4.6 0.270 (OA) vs 0.422 (CA) (Tables 3-4, Sec 4.2 'Thirdly').
74.6% of (response, constraint) pairs are annotated 'followed' (Sec 3.3) -- a baseline pass-rate/floor context for individual constraint checkers.
Preference-verification retention rate: only 71.2% of constructed candidate preference relations survive human unanimous-agreement filtering (Sec 3.2.2).
Annotation agreement: Cohen's Kappa 0.67 (initial instruction-following annotation) and 0.87 (cross-validation) (Sec 3.3, Appendix E.3); 0.95 raw agreement in cross-validation.

Inference-time scaling: disabling chain-of-thought/thinking mode substantially HURTS judge performance (GLM-4.6 constraint assessment tau_b drops 32.7%, Table 5); self-consistency majority voting (K up to 9) helps but saturates beyond K=7 (Sec 4.3, Table 5).
Complex composition types (Chain, Selection) are harder for judges to verify than simple types (Single, And) (Sec 4.2 'Performance on different constraint types', Fig. 4).

IF-RewardBench yields the lowest average pairwise judge accuracy among compared benchmarks (Fig. 6) and the strongest positive Somers' D correlation with downstream Best-of-8 sampling performance (Fig. 7, Sec 4.2/Appendix G.4), versus LLMBar/RewardBench-2 which are 'approaching saturation'.
Data curation cost: approximately $15,000 (Appendix E.4); 22 recruited annotators plus 5 professional data engineers for spot checks (Appendix E.2-E.3).
failure_modes: All general LLM judges show low negative-class F1 (N-F1) scores in constraint assessment (Table 3), indicating systematic under-detection of constraint VIOLATIONS -- judges are biased toward saying a constraint is followed even when it is not (Sec 4.2 'Firstly').

Judge performance is markedly worse when the instruction includes a system prompt or conversation history, especially for overall (non-checklist-guided) pairwise comparison; multi-turn and system-prompt-steerability instruction types are 'significantly more difficult than single-turn interaction' for overall assessment despite similar constraint-assessment performance (Sec 4.2 'Finally').

A specific documented failure: judges fail to correctly prioritize system-prompt constraints over conflicting later user-turn constraints -- an 'anomalous performance dip' occurs for instructions with 2 dialog turns and <3 constraints, traced to 'judge models failing to distinguish the priority of system prompts versus user prompts' (Sec 4.2 'Key factors', Appendix G.3, case studies Tables 13-14). In the Table 13 case study, all three shown judges (Gemini-3-Flash, GPT-5-mini, GLM-4.6) incorrectly favor the response that follows the user prompt over the golden-correct response that follows the conflicting system prompt (all pick [[A]] over the correct [[B]]); in the Table 14 case study, GPT-5-mini and GLM-4.6 again incorrectly favor the user-prompt-following response ([[B]]) while Gemini-3-Flash alone correctly favors the system-prompt-following response ([[A]], highlighted in blue as the one successful example across both tables).

Situation and Style constraint categories (subjective, context-dependent) show the largest performance gap vs. human judges, versus Numerical and Format constraints (objective, explicit) which judges handle relatively well (Sec 4.2 'Performance on different constraint types', Fig. 4).

More complex constraint COMPOSITION types -- Chain and Selection -- pose greater verification difficulty than Single/And composition (Sec 4.2, Fig. 4); this directly parallels c22's design bet that Selection composition resists easy/one-shot solving.
Judge performance degrades as constraint COUNT per instruction increases and as dialog TURN count increases (>= 4 turns) (Sec 4.2 'Key factors', Fig. 5).

Judge performance degrades as response QUALITY increases (responses from stronger generation models are harder to correctly rank/verify), attributed to higher quality and lower variance among strong-model outputs (Sec 4.2 'Key factors', last sentence).

Dedicated (non-general) reward models generalize poorly to instruction-following overall assessment, underperforming general LLM judges by a wide margin despite being purpose-built reward models (Sec 4.2 'Thirdly'; Table 4 shows all dedicated RMs below tau_b 0.2).

Disabling long-chain/thinking-mode reasoning causes a consistent, sometimes large, performance decline across all evaluated tasks (Table 5; GLM-4.6 constraint assessment drops 32.7%), showing judges rely heavily on extended reasoning to verify constraints correctly.
one_shot_vs_multistep: The paper's judge-model task is itself always a single forward pass per judgment (one call sees the full instruction + full checklist + response(s) and must emit all per-constraint verdicts or a pairwise verdict at once; Appendix G.2 explicitly favors 'a single inference pass' over per-constraint calls for cost efficiency and reasoning-model performance). The paper does NOT study an interactive multi-turn feedback loop for the judge itself (no revision-after-feedback protocol for judges). Where the paper does study 'more compute across a repeated process' is inference-time scaling of a SINGLE judgment (Sec 4.3, Table 5): (a) enabling long-chain-of-thought reasoning within one pass substantially helps (removing it hurts up to -32.7% tau_b); (b) self-consistency -- sampling K independent full judgments (K=1..9) and majority-voting -- improves performance monotonically but saturates beyond K=7, i.e., repeated independent one-shot judgments outperform a single one-shot judgment but with diminishing returns; there is no evidence in this paper of models improving via iterative interactive correction across turns with external feedback (that concept is out of scope for this benchmark, which measures judges, not generation-then-revision agents).

relation_to_candidate.position: IF-RewardBench sits on a different axis of the family than c22: it is a benchmark for JUDGE/REWARD models scoring already-generated responses against a constraint checklist and against each other (ranking), not a benchmark of a GENERATOR model producing an output under stacked constraints. Protocol mode is single-shot per judgment (full instruction + checklist + response(s) observed at once, Sec 3.1.1/Appendix G.2). What is observed: for constraint assessment, the full instruction (optionally with system prompt/history) + the complete constraint checklist + one candidate response; for overall assessment, the full instruction + a set of full candidate responses (pairwise, via ELO). Output shape: NOT a single fact/label in the sense of a generation target -- it is a vector of per-constraint binary verdicts (Eq. 1) for constraint assessment, or a ranking/pairwise preference for overall assessment; i.e., the model under test is a verifier/ranker, and its own output is structured (a checklist of verdicts or a total order), not a short generated string. Verifier type: hybrid -- automatic evaluation scripts where available (from IFBench, Multi-IF, IHEval; Appendix B.1) plus extensive human annotation for the rest (Sec 3.2.2), producing the GROUND TRUTH against which judge models are themselves scored; the paper is fundamentally a meta-evaluation of verifiers, whereas c22 is a task that USES a deterministic verifier (the IFBench checker library) to score a GENERATOR's output.
relation_to_candidate.overlap: Both draw directly from the same underlying constraint taxonomy and composition-type lineage: IF-RewardBench explicitly adopts ComplexBench's (Wen et al. 2024) And/Chain/Selection/Nested composition types (Table 9, Appendix A) -- the identical Selection ('choose the correct branch according to a condition, then follow that branch's constraints') mechanism that c22's dossier identifies as its core anti-one-shot-enumeration device. IF-RewardBench's own Table 9 example of Selection ('If the work contains any animal, describe in English; otherwise in Chinese') is structurally identical to c22's illustrative Selection atom (even-letter-count -> lowercase; else Title Case). Both also draw on IFBench (Pyatkin et al. 2025) as a direct source/ancestor (IF-RewardBench pulls 90 single-turn instructions from IFBench and reuses its evaluation scripts, Appendix B.1; c22 vendors IFBench's checker library directly). Both papers independently report that Chain/Selection composition is empirically HARDER than Single/And composition -- IF-RewardBench for JUDGES verifying constraint following (Sec 4.2, Fig. 4), which is indirect but relevant corroboration that Selection-style conditional constraints are non-trivial to reason about, supporting c22's premise that Selection constraints resist one-shot solving/enumeration.
relation_to_candidate.differences: IF-RewardBench (1) evaluates judge/reward models, not generator models -- there is no 'micro-generation' task solved under constraints; (2) scores are never strict all-or-nothing 0/1 pass/fail on a single response -- instead it computes a mean pass-rate r_y = (1/n)*sum(j_k) (Eq. 1, partial credit, the opposite of c22's 'all must pass' design) and, separately, ranks multiple responses relative to each other rather than scoring one response absolutely; (3) instructions are long, natural, multi-turn/system-prompt-laden real or LLM-synthesized instructions with an average of 5.38 constraints (Table 2) embedded in rich task content (blog posts, summaries, painting descriptions), not c22's deliberately trivial base micro-task; (4) constraints are decomposed via LLM + heavy human revision from naturalistic instructions (Sec 3.2.1, Appendix C Table 19), not programmatically composed from a fixed deterministic checker library with a seeded generator as in c22; (5) 'multi-turn' in IF-RewardBench denotes conversation-history-dependent instructions (an input-context axis), not an interactive generation-and-feedback loop as sometimes implied by 'multi-step' in other family members.
relation_to_candidate.what_c22_would_add: c22 would add: (a) a strict all-or-nothing (no-partial-credit) scoring regime absent from IF-RewardBench's mean-pass-rate formulation; (b) a genuinely trivial, contamination-resistant, infinitely-generatable base task (vs. IF-RewardBench's curated/synthesized naturalistic instructions with fixed, finite, released data); (c) evaluation of a GENERATOR (not a judge) directly against a deterministic checker, closing the loop that IF-RewardBench studies only from the judge side; (d) a reusable, already-unit-tested deterministic checker library (IFBench's) as the atom oracle, whereas IF-RewardBench's ground truth relies substantially on costly (~$15k) human annotation for constraint types (e.g., Situation, Style) that 'require deep understanding of social context' and that IF-RewardBench itself flags as ill-suited to binary automatic judgment (Sec 4.2, 'Performance on different constraint types'); c22 avoids this by restricting to the deterministically-checkable atom types (word count, forbidden letter, keyword, casing, end-token) that IF-RewardBench's own analysis shows are the EASIEST/most-reliable category (Numerical/Format constraints, Fig. 4) -- i.e., c22 deliberately occupies the well-behaved, fully-automatable corner of the constraint space that IF-RewardBench shows is necessary but insufficient for realistic instruction-following evaluation.

corpus_claim: IF-RewardBench (thu-coai, ACL 2026) provides 842 IF instructions with single-turn / multi-turn / system-prompt-steerability constraint coverage, extending the verifiable-constraint atom pool.
corpus_claim_verdict: partially-supported. The 842-instruction count, the three instruction types (single-turn interaction / multi-turn interaction / system-prompt steerability), and the thu-coai/ACL 2026 attribution are all directly confirmed in the paper (Table 1, Table 2, header/venue metadata, Sec 1 bullet 'Comprehensive Coverage'). However, the phrase 'extending the verifiable-constraint atom pool' is a loose characterization: IF-RewardBench is fundamentally a judge/reward-model meta-evaluation benchmark (it scores how well LLM judges rank and verify responses), not a pool of new deterministic-checker 'atoms' for generation tasks. Most of its constraint categories (Situation, Style, Action, and many Content/Linguistic subtypes) are explicitly NOT reliably code-verifiable and instead rely on human annotation or evaluation scripts borrowed from prior benchmarks (IFBench, Multi-IF, IHEval; Appendix B.1) -- the paper itself notes 'many hard constraints cannot be reliably verified using verification codes alone' (Appendix B.2). So while the benchmark does contain and catalog a broad constraint taxonomy (7 categories, 54 subcategories, 4 composition types incl. Selection; Appendix A, Table 8-9) that could inform/extend an atom pool conceptually, it does not itself ship a new library of deterministic checkers comparable to IFBench's; it is better described as extending the CONSTRAINT TAXONOMY / COMPOSITION-TYPE coverage and the JUDGE evaluation methodology rather than the verifiable-checker CODE pool per se.
baseline_relevant: Instance/difficulty dials actually used: average 5.38 constraints per instruction overall (5.52 single-turn, 4.50 multi-turn, 5.89 system-prompt; Table 2); dialog turns average 1.83 overall (1.00 single-turn by definition, 3.23 multi-turn, 2.01 system-prompt; Table 2). Difficulty scaling was explicitly studied and both HIGHER constraint counts and MORE dialog turns (>=4) degrade judge (and by extension task) performance (Sec 4.2 'Key factors', Fig. 5) -- directly relevant as an empirical justification for using constraint-count as an incremental difficulty dial, as c22 proposes.

Prompt format that measurably swings accuracy: giving the model the DECOMPOSED CONSTRAINT CHECKLIST (vs. just the raw instruction) measurably improves accuracy -- general LLM judges perform WORSE on 'overall assessment' (no checklist) than 'constraint assessment' (explicit checklist) (Sec 4.2 'Thirdly'). This supports c22's design of always surfacing an explicit, itemized constraint list (or per-checker diagnostic) rather than relying on the model to infer/enumerate constraints unaided -- though note the OPPOSITE point is c22's premise for Selection atoms: a checklist that reveals the constraint would defeat the 'discover, not copy' anti-enumeration mechanic, so this is a double-edged data point.

Metrics used: F1 for positive and negative classes (verification accuracy, capturing both over- and under-criticism) and Kendall tau_b (ranking correlation) (Sec 4.1). A strict all-or-nothing scorer (as in c22) is NOT used anywhere in this paper; IF-RewardBench instead always aggregates partial per-constraint pass rates (Eq. 1) -- useful as a documented ALTERNATIVE scoring convention to contrast against c22's chosen strict-AND design.

Floors/ceilings observed: constraint-level pass rate floor/ceiling context -- 74.6% of (response, constraint) pairs are annotated 'followed' overall (Sec 3.3), i.e., roughly a 3-in-4 base pass rate per SINGLE constraint from a mix of real LLM responses of varying quality; this is a useful empirical anchor for calibrating how many constraints can be stacked before an all-pass strict scorer floors near zero (if per-constraint pass probability is ~0.75, 4-5 independent constraints stacked strictly would already floor overall pass rate near 0.24-0.32 in expectation, consistent with c22's own stated risk of 'strict all-pass over many atoms can floor a small model near zero').

Contamination/data-release status: code and data are publicly released at https://github.com/thu-coai/IF-RewardBench (Abstract, header). The instructions are drawn substantially from 14 existing open-source instruction-following benchmarks plus LLM-synthesized instructions and real-application-scenario seeds (Table 10, Appendix B) -- i.e., NOT a fresh contamination-proof synthetic pool at inference time; by contrast, the paper's own downstream-correlation study (Sec 4.2, Appendix G.4) had to draw 300 FRESH instructions from an online chat platform specifically because 'many instruction-following benchmarks have been included in IF-RewardBench' and could be contaminated/already covered -- this is directly relevant precedent supporting c22's synthetic/infinite-pool design as a deliberate anti-contamination strategy.

Human annotation cost/scale as a cross-check: ~$15,000 total curation cost, 22 annotators + 5 data engineers, Cohen's Kappa 0.67 initial / 0.87 cross-validated (Appendix E) -- useful context for how expensive human-verified ground truth is for constraint types that are NOT deterministically checkable, reinforcing the value of c22's fully-automatic (code-only) oracle for the atom types it selects.

Constraint-category difficulty ranking directly informs which atom types are 'safe' to use for a deterministic-checker task: Numerical and Format constraints (word count, casing, keyword, end-token -- c22's exact atom set) are reported as the EASIEST/most reliably verified category by both judges and, implicitly, by verification code; Situation and Style constraints are the HARDEST and show the largest human-vs-judge gap, explicitly flagged as potentially 'ill-suited' to binary automatic judgment (Sec 4.2 'Performance on different constraint types', Fig. 4). This is strong corroborating evidence that c22's atom selection (avoiding subjective/style constraints in favor of Numerical/Format-type checkers) is well-founded.

Selection/Chain composition difficulty: judges perform worse on Chain and Selection composed constraints than Single/And (Sec 4.2, Fig. 4) -- corroborates, from an independent angle (judge reliability rather than generator difficulty), that c22's chosen Selection mechanism is a genuinely harder composition type worth using as an anti-enumeration device.
quotes: "Extensive experiments on IF-RewardBench reveal significant deficiencies in current judge models and demonstrate that our benchmark achieves a stronger positive correlation with downstream task performance compared to existing benchmarks." (Abstract)

"even the leading proprietary LLM, Gemini-3-Pro, achieves only a moderate Kendall correlation of 0.609 in ranking responses, significantly falling behind the human performance of 0.755. Top-tier open-source models, such as GLM-4.6 and Deepseek-V3.2, remain below or near 0.4, while all dedicated reward models fail to exceed 0.2." (Sec 1 / Abstract)

"Selection | The output is required to select the correct branch according to certain conditions, and then follow the constraints of this branch. | Please introduce the following painting. - If the work contains any animal, the description should be in English - Otherwise, the description should be in Chinese Painting: 'Mona Lisa'" (Table 9, Appendix A)

"more complex constraint composition types Chain and Selection pose greater challenges to judge models compared to the simpler Single and And types." (Sec 4.2, 'Performance on different constraint types')
"general LLMs perform worse on overall assessment than constraint assessment, likely due to the difficulty of identifying constraints within complex instructions." (Sec 4.2, 'Main Results, Thirdly')
"Data examination reveals this degradation stems from judge models failing to distinguish the priority of system prompts versus user prompts, as detailed in Appendix G.3." (Sec 4.2, 'Key factors')

"it is notable that many hard constraints cannot be reliably verified using verification codes alone, such as constraints targeting for specific segments of the response ... and the composition of soft and hard constraints ... as it is challenging for verification code to accurately extract the corresponding segments of the response that should follow these constraints." (Appendix B.2)
"We spend approximately 15,000$ on the curation of IF-RewardBench." (Appendix E.4)

corrections: Table numbering error: protocol.description misattributed the pairwise/MT-Bench-style prompt to 'Table 20' -- the paper's actual Table 20 (per its own caption, which follows its content block) is the constraint-assessment prompt ('You are an impartial judge... think carefully and provide a detailed analysis of whether the final round assistant response follows each constraint'); the pairwise-comparison prompt ('Please act as an impartial judge and evaluate the quality of the responses provided by two AI assistants...') is Table 21. Fixed the description to cite Table 20 for constraint assessment and Table 21 for pairwise/overall assessment, and corrected the paraphrased constraint-assessment output format (was rendered as informal '[Constraint N]'/'[[follows]]'/'[[does not follow]]', which does not match the paper's actual format strings '[The Start of Constraint N]' and '[[The AI assistant's response follows/does not follow this constraint]]').

Table numbering error: protocol.description cited 'Tables 15, 17, 18' as the prompts containing in-context-example placeholders. Verified against source: Table 15 (checklist revision, has '{examples}') and Table 17 (quality scoring, has '{in_context_examples}') do contain such placeholders, but Table 18 (difficulty-scoring prompt) does NOT -- it has no examples placeholder at all. The human instruction-following annotation guideline (Table 16, a human annotator guideline, not an LLM judge prompt) does have '{in-context examples}' and was omitted from the original list. Corrected to cite Tables 15, 16, and 17, and to explicitly note Table 18 (and Table 19, instruction decomposition) have no such placeholder.

Model entry error (Gemini-3-Flash): the dossier claimed Gemini-3-Flash was 'still the top score' on the Appendix G.3 / Table 6 cross-benchmark (IHEval-subset) overall-assessment comparison, citing tau_b=0.161. Verified against Table 6 in the source: Gemini-3-Flash=0.161 is actually the SECOND-highest score in that table; GPT-5-mini scores higher at tau_b=0.211 (the actual top score; nearly all other judges are negative in this subset). Corrected both the Gemini-3-Flash and GPT-5-mini model entries to reflect the true ranking.
